examples.title: Binary Search Implementation
examples.description: Iterative implementation of binary search with complete complexity analysis
examples.code:
// Binary Search: Iterative Implementation
// Input: Sorted array A[0..n-1], key to search
// Output: Index of key if found, -1 otherwise

FUNCTION BinarySearch(A, n, key):
    low ← 0
    high ← n - 1
    
    WHILE low ≤ high DO:
        mid ← (low + high) / 2
        
        IF A[mid] = key THEN:
            RETURN mid        // Key found
        ELSE IF A[mid] < key THEN:
            low ← mid + 1     // Search right half
        ELSE:
            high ← mid - 1    // Search left half
        END IF
    END WHILE
    
    RETURN -1               // Key not found
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: The algorithm maintains a search window [low, high]. Each iteration calculates the middle index and compares with key. If key equals middle, return found. If key is greater, search right half (low = mid + 1). If key is smaller, search left half (high = mid - 1). Loop terminates when key is found or window becomes empty (low > high).

examples.title: Merge Two Sorted Arrays
examples.description: Algorithm to merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array with complexity analysis
examples.code:
// Merge Operation: Combine two sorted arrays
// Input: Array A[0..m-1], Array B[0..n-1] (both sorted)
// Output: Merged sorted array C[0..m+n-1]

FUNCTION Merge(A, m, B, n):
    CREATE Array C of size m+n
    i ← 0    // Pointer for A
    j ← 0    // Pointer for B
    k ← 0    // Pointer for C
    
    // Compare and merge
    WHILE i < m AND j < n DO:
        IF A[i] ≤ B[j] THEN:
            C[k] ← A[i]
            i ← i + 1
        ELSE:
            C[k] ← B[j]
            j ← j + 1
        END IF
        k ← k + 1
    END WHILE
    
    // Copy remaining elements from A (if any)
    WHILE i < m DO:
        C[k] ← A[i]
        i ← i + 1
        k ← k + 1
    END WHILE
    
    // Copy remaining elements from B (if any)
    WHILE j < n DO:
        C[k] ← B[j]
        j ← j + 1
        k ← k + 1
    END WHILE
    
    RETURN C
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: The merge algorithm uses three pointers to traverse both input arrays and the result array. It compares elements from both arrays and copies the smaller one to the result. When one array is exhausted, remaining elements from the other are copied. This is the fundamental operation used in Merge Sort.

examples.title: Linear Search with Count
examples.description: Linear search implementation returning the number of comparisons made
examples.explanation: This linear search implementation counts comparisons to analyze performance. Each iteration increments the comparison counter before checking equality. This demonstrates how we can track actual comparisons to verify theoretical complexity analysis. The function returns the index where key is found or -1 if not present.
